name: ECO4 (Energy Company Obligation 4)
fundingNotes: Funding level determined by energy suppliers and property assessment; no fixed cap published. Verify with the provider.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists ECO4 as an obligation on larger energy suppliers to fund energy-efficiency improvements in low-income and fuel-poor homes. The scheme states that properties must be owner-occupied or privately rented and meet income/benefit criteria.
eligibilitySummary: Property must be in England, Scotland, or Wales
The scheme states tenants or owners must receive qualifying means-tested benefits, or the property must score low on a LA flex referral
EPC rating D–G typically required (verify with supplier)
Private rented sector properties may apply where tenants meet benefit criteria
Landlord must agree to installation access
howToApply: Contact a participating energy supplier (e.g. British Gas, E.ON, EDF, Octopus)
Or approach an Ofgem-registered ECO installer who can refer the property
A home assessment will be arranged to confirm eligibility
Verify current eligibility criteria on the official Ofgem page before acting

name: Great British Insulation Scheme (GBIS)
fundingNotes: Single insulation measure funded per property; no fixed monetary cap published by Ofgem. Verify with the provider.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists GBIS as a separate obligation from ECO4, focused on delivering a single insulation measure to properties in the lower EPC bands. The scheme states it targets both low-income households and a broader group based on EPC band alone.
eligibilitySummary: Property must be in England, Scotland, or Wales
EPC band D–G required for the low-income group; band D or E for the general group
The scheme states one insulation measure per property is funded
Private landlords may apply where tenants meet income criteria or property meets EPC threshold
Verify current eligibility on the official Ofgem page
howToApply: Contact a participating energy supplier or GBIS-registered installer
Provide EPC certificate and, where applicable, evidence of qualifying benefits
A surveyor will confirm which insulation measure is appropriate
Verify current scheme status on the Ofgem website before acting

name: Boiler Upgrade Scheme (BUS)
provider: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
fundingNotes: The official page lists £7,500 for air-source heat pumps and biomass boilers; £7,500 for ground-source heat pumps (verify current figures on GOV.UK as rates have changed historically).
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Boiler Upgrade Scheme as providing upfront capital grants to replace fossil-fuel boilers with low-carbon heating. The scheme states it is open to homeowners and landlords in England and Wales.
eligibilitySummary: Property must be in England or Wales
An MCS-certified installer must be used; they apply on behalf of the property owner
The scheme states existing heating system must be replaced (not supplemented)
Properties with valid EPC and no outstanding recommendations for loft/cavity insulation (or evidence measures are unsuitable)
Landlords and private owners both listed as eligible — verify with the provider
howToApply: Find an MCS-certified heat-pump installer via the MCS installer database
The installer checks eligibility and submits the voucher application to DESNZ
Voucher is issued before installation begins
Verify current grant levels and eligibility at GOV.UK before commissioning work

name: Home Upgrade Grant Phase 2 (HUG2)
provider: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) via local authorities
fundingNotes: Funding delivered via local authority consortia; no single national cap. Verify current availability with your local authority.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists HUG2 as targeting low-income households in properties with an EPC rating of E, F, or G that are off the gas grid. Funding is administered through local authority consortia in England.
eligibilitySummary: England only
The scheme states properties must be EPC-rated E, F, or G
Households must meet low-income or fuel-poverty criteria
Property must be off the gas grid (no gas central heating) — verify with local authority
Private rented and owner-occupied properties may apply
howToApply: Contact your local authority to find out if they are participating in HUG2
Apply through the local authority or their delivery partner
A home survey will assess which measures are appropriate
Verify current round status on GOV.UK before acting

name: Warm Homes: Social Housing Fund
fundingNotes: Wave 3 funding levels and allocations are confirmed via competitive application to Homes England. Verify current round status with the provider.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Warm Homes: Social Housing Fund (formerly the Social Housing Decarbonisation Fund) as providing capital grants to social landlords to upgrade the energy performance of their stock to EPC band C or above.
eligibilitySummary: England only
Applicants must be registered providers of social housing or local authority landlords
The scheme states target properties must be EPC D or below
Applications made via competitive bidding to Homes England
Tenant benefit criteria may apply — verify with Homes England
howToApply: Monitor Homes England and DESNZ for open funding rounds
Submit an expression of interest or full application via the Homes England portal
Appoint a delivery partner/contractor meeting scheme requirements
Verify current round status and guidance on the official page before acting

name: Home Energy Scotland
provider: Energy Saving Trust (on behalf of Scottish Government)
fundingNotes: The official page lists both grants and interest-free loans; amounts vary by measure and household income. Verify current figures with Home Energy Scotland directly.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists Home Energy Scotland as offering free energy advice plus grants and loans for home energy improvements in Scotland. The scheme states it covers a wide range of measures including insulation, heating, and renewables.
eligibilitySummary: Scotland only
Available to homeowners and some private landlords — verify current landlord eligibility with Home Energy Scotland
Income-based and universal strands exist; criteria vary by measure
The scheme states properties must be permanently occupied as a main residence (owner-occupier rules apply)
howToApply: Call Home Energy Scotland on 0808 808 2282 (freephone) or apply online
A free home assessment will identify suitable measures
Grants and/or loans are offered based on household and property criteria
Verify current eligibility and funding levels on the official page before acting

name: Nest Wales
fundingNotes: The official page lists Nest as providing free energy efficiency improvements; no fixed monetary cap is published. Verify with the provider.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists Nest as Wales's national scheme offering free home energy improvements to households on low incomes or benefits. The scheme states it covers insulation, heating, and renewable measures.
eligibilitySummary: Wales only
The scheme states householders must receive a qualifying means-tested benefit, or have a health condition affected by cold homes
Private rented properties may be included where tenants meet criteria — verify with Nest Wales
EPC band E, F, or G typically required
howToApply: Apply online at nest.gov.wales or call 0808 808 2244
Provide benefit entitlement evidence and property details
A free home survey is arranged to identify eligible measures
Verify current eligibility on the official Welsh Government page before acting

name: NI Sustainable Energy Programme (NISEP)
provider: Northern Ireland Sustainable Energy Association (NI-SEA) / Utility Regulator
fundingNotes: Funding is delivered through energy suppliers operating in Northern Ireland; amounts vary by scheme strand and year. Verify with NI-SEA or your energy supplier.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists NISEP as Northern Ireland's main energy-efficiency obligation scheme, funded by electricity suppliers. The scheme states it offers grants for insulation, heating upgrades, and related measures to eligible households.
eligibilitySummary: Northern Ireland only
The scheme states households must receive qualifying benefits or meet low-income criteria
Private rented properties may qualify where tenants meet criteria — verify with the provider
Participating electricity suppliers administer delivery
howToApply: Contact NI-SEA or a participating electricity supplier in Northern Ireland
Provide evidence of qualifying benefits and property details
An assessment will confirm eligible measures
Verify current scheme status and eligibility on the official page before acting

name: Disabled Facilities Grant (England)
fundingNotes: The official page lists a mandatory grant of up to £30,000 in England. Local authorities may top up beyond this figure — verify with your local authority.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Disabled Facilities Grant as a mandatory grant from local housing authorities for adaptations to help disabled people live independently. The scheme states it applies to properties where a disabled person lives or intends to live.
eligibilitySummary: England only
The scheme states the grant is for properties where a disabled person (tenant or owner) lives or will live
Works must be recommended by an occupational therapist
Landlords must agree to the works and comply with conditions
A means test applies to the disabled person's finances (not the landlord's)
howToApply: Contact your local housing authority's DFG team
Request an occupational therapist assessment through the local authority or NHS
Complete the formal grant application with supporting evidence
Verify current maximum grant levels and means-test thresholds with your local authority

name: Scheme of Assistance (Scotland)
fundingNotes: Funding levels vary by local authority and individual assessment. Verify with your local council.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Scheme of Assistance as Scotland's framework for helping homeowners and tenants fund essential repairs and adaptations. The scheme states local authorities must provide information and assistance, which may include grants for adaptations.
eligibilitySummary: Scotland only
The scheme states local authorities are obliged to assist homeowners with essential repairs and adaptations
Mandatory grants exist for adaptations to meet the needs of a disabled person
Private landlords may need to apply separately — verify with your local authority
Means-testing and criteria vary by local authority
howToApply: Contact your local council's housing or private rented sector team
Request an assessment for the specific adaptation or repair needed
Complete the local authority application process
Verify current grant availability and criteria on your council's website

name: Enable / Rapid Response Adaptations (Wales)
provider: Care and Repair Cymru / Welsh Government
fundingNotes: The official page lists small rapid adaptations at low or no cost; larger adaptations may be funded via the Disabled Facilities Grant. Verify with Care and Repair or your local authority.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists Enable and Rapid Response Adaptations as Welsh Government programmes delivered through Care and Repair agencies, providing small adaptations and repairs to help older and disabled people stay safely at home.
eligibilitySummary: Wales only
The scheme states it primarily targets older people and those with disabilities or long-term health conditions
Minor adaptations (grab rails, ramps) may be provided at no cost under Rapid Response
Larger adaptations may be referred to the Disabled Facilities Grant process
Private rented properties may be covered — verify with local Care and Repair agency
howToApply: Contact your local Care and Repair agency or local authority
An assessment will identify the adaptations needed
Rapid Response aims for quick turnaround for minor items
Verify current scope and eligibility on the Care and Repair Cymru website

name: Disabled Facilities Grant (Northern Ireland)
fundingNotes: The official page lists grant levels for Northern Ireland; amounts differ from the England DFG. Verify current figures with NIHE.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists NIHE's Disabled Facilities Grant as funding adaptations to enable disabled people to remain in or access their home. The scheme states it is available to owner-occupiers and private tenants in Northern Ireland.
eligibilitySummary: Northern Ireland only
The scheme states the property must be the main residence of the disabled person
Works must be necessary and appropriate, as confirmed by an occupational therapist
A means test applies to the disabled person or household
Verify current grant limits with NIHE before acting
howToApply: Contact NIHE on 03448 920 900 or visit a local office
Obtain an occupational therapist referral
Submit the formal application with supporting documentation
Verify current eligibility and grant levels on the NIHE website

name: Empty Homes Grant (Wales)
provider: Welsh local authorities (e.g. Rhondda Cynon Taf, others)
fundingNotes: Schemes are run at local authority level; amounts and criteria differ significantly between councils. Verify with your local authority.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists local authority empty homes grants in Wales as funding to bring long-term empty properties back into use. The scheme states availability, amounts, and conditions vary by council.
eligibilitySummary: Wales only
The scheme states properties must have been empty for a minimum period (varies by council, often 6–12 months)
Owner must commit to bringing the property back into use, often as affordable rented accommodation
Conditions on rental period and tenant type may apply — verify with your local authority
Not all Welsh councils operate a dedicated empty homes grant — check with your council
howToApply: Contact your local council's housing or empty homes team
Request information on any current empty homes grant or loan scheme
Submit an application with property ownership evidence and renovation plans
Verify current availability and conditions with your local authority before acting

name: Scotland Empty Homes Partnership
fundingNotes: The Partnership itself does not disburse grants directly; it signposts owners to local authority funding. Verify current local authority grant availability with your council.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Scotland Empty Homes Partnership as providing advice and support to bring long-term empty properties back into use. The scheme states it works with local councils who may have loan or grant schemes.
eligibilitySummary: Scotland only
The Partnership advises owners of properties empty for six months or more
Local authority funding availability varies — the Partnership can signpost to relevant council schemes
Some councils operate loan or grant schemes for renovation of empty homes
Verify current local authority funding with your council or the Partnership
howToApply: Contact the Scotland Empty Homes Partnership via Shelter Scotland
Or contact your local council's empty homes officer directly
Receive advice on options including grants, loans, leasing, or sale
Verify current funding options on the official page before acting

name: Historic England Repair Grants
fundingNotes: Grant levels depend on the significance of the building and the works required. The official page lists schemes at national and local level. Verify with Historic England.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists Historic England's repair grants as targeting at-risk listed buildings and scheduled monuments in England. The scheme states grants are prioritised for the most significant and vulnerable heritage assets.
eligibilitySummary: England only
Property must be listed (Grade I or II*) or a scheduled monument, or be in a conservation area at risk
The scheme states priority is given to buildings on the Heritage at Risk Register
Grants are typically partial contributions; match funding is usually required
Verify current programmes, criteria, and open rounds with Historic England
howToApply: Check the Historic England website for open grant programmes relevant to your building
Contact your local Historic England office for pre-application advice
Submit a full application with conservation management plan and costs
Verify current availability and criteria on the Historic England website before acting

name: National Lottery Heritage Fund
fundingNotes: The official page lists grants from £3,000 upward. Large capital grants for significant heritage projects can reach several million pounds. Verify current programme levels on the Fund's website.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the National Lottery Heritage Fund as the largest dedicated funder of UK heritage, covering buildings, landscapes, collections, and culture. The scheme states applicants must demonstrate public benefit and heritage significance.
eligibilitySummary: UK-wide
Applicants are typically organisations (charities, trusts, local authorities) rather than individual landlords
The scheme states the heritage significance and public benefit must be clearly demonstrated
Match funding is usually required for larger grants
Verify current open programmes, priorities, and eligibility on the Fund's website
howToApply: Review current open programmes on the National Lottery Heritage Fund website
Register and submit an Expression of Interest via their online portal
Develop a full application if invited, with business case, heritage significance, and project plan
Verify current programme criteria and deadlines before acting

name: Listed Places of Worship Grant Scheme
provider: Department for Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS)
fundingNotes: The scheme refunds VAT paid on eligible repair and maintenance works to listed places of worship. The official page lists the current VAT refund mechanism. Verify with DCMS.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Listed Places of Worship Grant Scheme as refunding VAT on eligible repair and maintenance works to listed places of worship across the UK. The scheme states it is available to groups using buildings primarily as places of worship.
eligibilitySummary: UK-wide
The building must be listed and used primarily as a place of worship
The scheme states only eligible repair and maintenance works qualify (not alterations or extensions)
The grant refunds the VAT element of the contractor's invoice
Verify eligible works and current scheme operation on the official page
howToApply: Register the place of worship with the scheme
Ensure works are carried out by a VAT-registered contractor
Submit a claim with VAT invoices and supporting documentation
Verify current eligibility criteria and process on the official DCMS page

name: Affordable Homes Programme 2021–26
fundingNotes: The programme allocates capital grant per unit; figures vary by tenure type, location, and scheme. Verify current rates with Homes England.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Affordable Homes Programme 2021–26 as providing capital grant to registered providers and others to deliver new affordable homes in England. The scheme states it targets social rent, affordable rent, and shared ownership.
eligibilitySummary: England only
Applicants must be registered providers, local authorities, or approved development partners
The scheme states homes must meet Homes England's design and tenure requirements
Grant levels per unit depend on tenure type and location
Verify current programme status and allocation with Homes England
howToApply: Register as a Homes England investment partner
Submit a strategic partnership or continuous market engagement (CME) application
Meet design, sustainability, and tenure requirements
Verify current programme availability and criteria on the Homes England website

name: Social and Affordable Homes Programme 2026–36
fundingNotes: The programme was announced in the 2025 Spending Review. Detailed grant rates and eligibility criteria are subject to Homes England's published prospectus. Verify current details with Homes England.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Social and Affordable Homes Programme 2026–36 as the successor to AHP 2021–26, with a stated ambition of delivering 1.5 million new homes. The scheme states it covers social rent, affordable rent, and shared ownership across England.
eligibilitySummary: England only (GLA administers London allocations separately)
The scheme states applicants must be Homes England investment partners or GLA partners
Homes must meet design quality, sustainability, and tenure requirements
Full eligibility criteria to be confirmed in the official programme prospectus
Verify current programme details with Homes England before acting
howToApply: Monitor Homes England and GLA websites for the official programme prospectus
Register or update your investment partner status with Homes England
Submit an application in line with published guidance when rounds open
Verify current status and criteria on the Homes England website before acting

name: Warm Homes: Local Grant
provider: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ) via local authorities
fundingNotes: The official page lists this as the successor to LAD and HUG schemes; grant levels are determined by local authority delivery plans. Verify current availability with your local authority.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Warm Homes: Local Grant as funding administered through local authorities in England to improve energy efficiency in low-income private and owner-occupied homes. The scheme states it targets properties with an EPC rating of E, F, or G.
eligibilitySummary: England only
The scheme states households must meet low-income or benefit-receipt criteria
Properties must be EPC-rated E, F, or G
Administered locally — availability varies by local authority area
Verify current local delivery with your council before acting
howToApply: Contact your local authority to find out whether the Warm Homes: Local Grant is active in your area
Apply through the council or its delivery partner
A home survey will assess eligible measures
Verify current scheme status and criteria on GOV.UK before acting

name: Smart Export Guarantee (SEG)
fundingNotes: SEG is a tariff (payment per kWh exported) not a capital grant. Export rates vary by supplier and tariff. The official page lists minimum obligations; verify current rates with your chosen supplier.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Smart Export Guarantee as requiring larger electricity suppliers to offer a tariff for renewable electricity exported to the grid from small-scale generators. The scheme states it applies to solar PV, wind, micro-CHP, hydro, and anaerobic digestion.
eligibilitySummary: Great Britain only (England, Scotland, Wales)
The scheme states the installation must be MCS-certified (or equivalent)
Capacity must be 5MW or below (or 50kW for micro-CHP)
The SEG is a payment mechanism, not a capital grant — it does not fund installation costs
Verify current export tariff rates with your chosen electricity supplier
howToApply: Install an eligible MCS-certified renewable system
Apply to a SEG-licensed electricity supplier for an export tariff
Ensure a suitable smart or export meter is installed
Compare rates between suppliers — the official page lists minimum eligibility obligations

name: Industrial Energy Transformation Fund (IETF)
provider: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
fundingNotes: The official page lists grants for feasibility studies and capital investment. Grant rates and caps vary by phase and project size. Verify current figures with DESNZ.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the Industrial Energy Transformation Fund as supporting businesses with high energy use to reduce consumption and transition to low-carbon processes. The scheme states it is aimed at energy-intensive industries, not domestic property.
eligibilitySummary: England only
The scheme states applicants must be businesses with significant industrial energy consumption
Projects must demonstrate meaningful energy savings or decarbonisation
Not designed for domestic residential property — included here as a signpost for commercial property owners
Verify current eligibility criteria and open rounds with DESNZ
howToApply: Monitor DESNZ for open IETF funding rounds
Submit an application via the published IETF portal with energy audit evidence
Feasibility grant phase may precede a full capital application
Verify current scheme status and eligibility on GOV.UK before acting

name: GLA Affordable Homes Programme
provider: Greater London Authority (GLA) / Mayor of London
fundingNotes: The official page lists the GLA as allocating capital grant to deliver affordable homes in London. Grant rates per unit are set by the GLA and vary by tenure type and location within London. Verify current rates with the GLA.
shortDescription: The official scheme page lists the GLA Affordable Homes Programme as the Mayor of London's investment in affordable housing in the capital, running alongside the national Homes England programme. The scheme states it funds social rent, affordable rent, and shared ownership homes.
eligibilitySummary: London only (properties must be within Greater London)
The scheme states applicants must be GLA investment partners (registered providers, local authorities, or approved developers)
Homes must meet the Mayor's design standards and tenure requirements
Grant rates and requirements differ from Homes England's national programme
Verify current programme status and criteria on the GLA website before acting
howToApply: Register as a GLA investment partner via the GLA's homes and communities portal
Submit an expression of interest or application in response to a GLA prospectus or call for projects
Meet design quality, sustainability, and tenure requirements set by the GLA
Verify current programme availability and grant rates on the official GLA page before acting
